items.content:
RT @Unveiled_ChinaX: A veteran clean energy scientist got caught copying 2,602 sensitive government files right before his retirement, and federal intelligence reports state the data was intended for Beijing. Dennis Lu, a senior researcher who spent decades developing advanced carbon capture technologies at Canada’s federal energy labs, is now headed to a high-stakes jury trial after his lifetime of access unraveled into a national security nightmare.

What makes this breach deeply unsettling is how long the warning signs were ignored. Court records reveal that CSIS, Canada's premier intelligence agency, formally warned his department about Lu at least three times starting all the way back in 2000. Despite these security flags, he retained his access for over two decades, allegedly sending nearly 2,000 work emails to his personal account while in China before executing mass downloads onto external devices in July and August of 2023.

While Western nations like the US and Canada invest billions into developing clean energy tech to protect future infrastructure, the CCP aggressively exploits systemic gaps through deceptive recruitment and espionage to simply take it. Lu's defense team argues he had an expectation of privacy at work and was completing routine academic collaborations, but federal prosecutors point to an unauthorized, state-sponsored effort to harvest intellectual property.

https://t.co/2Oh5sJqtN2

items.author: artem_i_baranov

items.content:
RT @ptdbugs: A PoC/exploit has been discovered for vulnerability in Firefox 152.0.5

Vendor: Mozilla Foundation 
Product: Mozilla Firefox
Description: Firefox 152.0.5 on Windows contains a chain of flaws that together allow full remote code execution from a single malicious webpage visit. A memory-safety issue in the WebAssembly/Baseline-JIT engine permits an attacker to break out of the browser's content-process sandbox into a privileged internal browser context. From there, insufficient origin/authorization checks in Firefox's profile-backup restore feature allow a maliciously crafted backup archive to be accepted and restored without proper validation, including an attacker-controlled NSS (cryptography module) configuration. On restart, NSS loads a library path from this restored configuration without verifying its authenticity, resulting in arbitrary code execution under the logged-in user's privileges. No user interaction beyond visiting a webpage is required, and no prior access, credentials, or privileges are needed — making this an unauthenticated, network-exploitable, one-click RCE chain.

Link: https://t.co/zBGxcSuBZH
items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @ptdbugs: A PoC/exploit has been discovered for vulnerability in OpenSSH 10.4p1

Vendor: OpenBSD
Product: OpenSSH 
Description: Portable OpenSSH's ssh-agent (affecting versions from 9.3p2 through at least 10.4p1) contains a state-tracking flaw in how it classifies forwarded versus local connections when the agent is locked and later unlocked. Because the session-bind@openssh.com extension used to mark a socket as "remote" is discarded outright while the agent is locked, a forwarded agent socket that remains open across a lock/unlock cycle is subsequently misclassified as local. This allows a party with access to that forwarded socket — typically an SSH server the victim connected to with agent forwarding enabled — to submit a PKCS#11 provider-loading request that the agent's remote-provider restriction was specifically designed to block, resulting in the agent loading and initializing an attacker-influenced module. Exploitation requires the victim to use agent forwarding to an attacker-influenced host, lock and later unlock their agent while that forwarded connection remains open, and a PKCS#11 provider path permitted by the agent's allowlist.

Link: https://t.co/AihqL5e7d7

items.content:
RT @ThruntingLabs: Most security training starts by telling you what happened.

Then it asks you to find the evidence.

Threat Hunting Labs works the other way around.

You begin with an alert, a small amount of context, and access to the telemetry.

From there, you have to:

• Form a hypothesis
• Write the queries
• Follow the evidence
• Decide what happened
• Explain how you reached that conclusion

The goal is not to memorize attacker techniques.

It is to become better at investigating them.

See how it works and start with free access: https://t.co/9sDdyIFHwE

items.content:
RT @cr3ghost: If you are into BYOVD exploitation, EDR killer drivers, credential dumping, physical memory manipulation, or building detections against any of it, read this.

Microsoft blocked MmMapIoSpace for page table reads in 1803. BYOVD drivers that relied on VA to PA resolution broke overnight.

Superfetch never got patched. One NtQuerySystemInformation call dumps the entire PFN database from userland. Every physical page. Every virtual address. Every owning process. No driver needed just to resolve the addresses.

Pair that with any read-write physical memory primitive and you have credential dumping, process manipulation, EDR tampering, and DMA attack setup.

Whether you are building detections around this or not, someone else already is using it.

https://t.co/e2HbV3E72m

Author: @OutflankNL 

#WindowsInternals #ThreatIntel #InfoSec

items.author: NetAskari

items.content:
Most people look at a debugger and immediately focus on the assembly instructions. I usually spend just as much time watching everything around them. Loaded modules, register changes, memory regions, thread activity, and the execution path often reveal far more about a sample than the code itself. Malware rarely gives away its real intentions in the first few instructions.

In this x64dbg session, the sample is paused inside ntdll.dll, which is where many malware families eventually end up before making Windows system calls. Watching how execution flows into and out of these low level APIs can expose process injection, memory allocation, privilege changes, or attempts to evade user mode monitoring. The interesting part is rarely where the malware starts. It is where it chooses to go next.

One habit that has made me a better malware analyst is correlating what I see in the debugger with Procmon, Process Explorer, API Monitor, Wireshark, and memory forensics. Looking at only one tool gives you one piece of the puzzle. Combining several viewpoints tells the complete story.

Reverse engineering is not about reading assembly faster than everyone else. It is about asking the right questions, validating every assumption, and following the evidence until the malware has nowhere left to hide. That mindset separates analysts who identify indicators from those who truly understand the threat.

items.author: 0xocdsec

items.content:
RT @cr3ghost: LLMs can now autonomously generate fully functional Mythic C2 agents from a single prompt. Built, tested, and deployed in under 2 hours. No human in the loop.

SpecterOps built a framework called Oracle that takes a prompt, generates the agent code, compiles it, deploys it to a Mythic server, tests every command against a live Windows target, runs QA validation, and ships a release build.

Tested across Python, Go, Zig, C#, and Rust. Every generated agent is unique and disposable.

If every engagement gets a fresh LLM-generated agent, static signatures and YARA rules are fundamentally unable to keep up. Defenders need to rethink detection around behaviour, not artifacts.

https://t.co/pDxFso5wtk

Author: @_xpn_ @SpecterOps 

#RedTeam #ThreatIntel #InfoSec
items.author: cr3ghost

items.content:
RT @cr3ghost: LLMs can now reverse engineer obfuscated malware. But what happens when malware authors use LLMs to iteratively obfuscate back?

Elastic Security Labs explored the arms race between LLM-driven reverse engineering and LLM-driven obfuscation. Who wins when both sides have the same tools?

This is the question every malware analyst, detection engineer, and red or blue teamer needs to be thinking about right now.

https://t.co/QURyXMRDcP

Author: @elasticseclabs 

#MalwareAnalysis #ReverseEngineering #InfoSec

items.content:
At some point we might know the truth, but we lack the tools to prove the truth itself, so it just flows like wind.

Many such cases in life.

And still, we keep seeking for the unseekeable.

I would say that's what makes us human, but... I cannot prove it.
items.author: zuhaitz_dev

items.content:
But, yeah, point is, many concepts, we explore them and seek for the truth, while some are metaphysical (outside of physics, the universe, literally).

And, you cannot prove it, you can believe but you cannot prove.

So, what a headache.

And...

items.content:
Man this Copilot feature is funny as hell. It's supposed to tell you why your Windows 11 PC dragging ass, but the app itself sitting there eating up to 1GB RAM idle cuz they stuffed a whole private Edge browser in it. Bold as fuck.  Cool idea for regular people who ain't tryna open Task Manager every time shit slow, but Microsoft need to stop with this bloat. Cut the webview bullshit, make it actually light like it used to be, and optimize instead of just stacking more AI on top of broken shit. Opt-in is whatever, performance still trash for what it is.

Respectfully.

items.author: 0xpwnie

items.content:
RT @vxunderground: Nah, I have to share some of my personal experiences for a second.

I saw a few newer people in information security share some code, or ideas they had, on social media. They retracted their posts, or code, because of criticism they received. This wasn't one singular person, to my surprise I saw like, five noobies sharing stuff.

Very cool.

Let me tell you something, stinky new people: yes, people online are mean. They are very mean. I have been BOILED ALIVE by stinky nerds online. I have been called every synonym for idiot you can imagine over the past ... uhhh, 21 years of doing malware related stuff.

And because this is the internet, and we're connected across the globe, I've had the pleasure of being called an idiot (or something similar) in English, Spanish, French, Russian, Mandarin, Hindi, Arabic, Portuguese, and more.

While these people are not nice, I very sincerely recommend filtering out the negative verbiage and focusing on the underlying message (if applicable). It is a skill that you'll need to adapt and grow in this field. In the "hacker" sphere, intellectuality is like, the thingie people value the most (or at least claim to).

Hence, if you share something it will be heavily critiqued. When you share something, whether you explicitly say it or not, you're inviting your peers for feedback. You WILL receive feedback, good or bad, and you will have to learn to handle it, process it, or learn from it.

Someone say your code is trash? Good. That is an opportunity to improve.

Someone criticize you for leaning heavily on AI? Good. Use AI as a tool to learn and stop using it as much.

Someone misunderstood what you said and called you a bad name? Good. Use that as an opportunity to practice improving your phrasing and writing.

Someone say your idea is repetitive and has been done before? Good, you discovered an idea for yourself, learned about an existing idea, and now you know how to find more ideas.

items.content:
🎣 𝗛𝗶𝗴𝗵 𝗙𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗙𝗼𝗿𝗴𝟯𝟲𝟱 𝗣𝗵𝗮𝗮𝗦 𝗗𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻

Building on the ZeroBec threat research into Forg365, I’ve uncovered additional backend nodes actively performing 𝗱𝗲𝘃𝗶𝗰𝗲 𝗰𝗼𝗱𝗲 𝗿𝗲𝗴𝗶𝘀𝘁𝗿𝗮𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻. These findings provide defenders with high-value intelligence on Forg365’s infrastructure.

https://t.co/qeZjNMN9Zc

To operationalize this, I’ve developed a 𝗵𝗶𝗴𝗵-𝗳𝗶𝗱𝗲𝗹𝗶𝘁𝘆 𝗱𝗲𝘁𝗲𝗰𝘁𝗶𝗼𝗻 leveraging 𝗘𝗻𝘁𝗿𝗮𝗜𝗱𝗦𝗶𝗴𝗻𝗜𝗻𝗘𝘃𝗲𝗻𝘁𝘀 focused on device code registration patterns linked to Forg365 PhaaS. This detection helps defenders spot and disrupt Forg365’s abuse of Entra sign-in flows with precision.

https://t.co/p6OuRkBZ0M

#Cybersecurity #PhaaS #Forg365 #ThreatDetection
